title: Oliguria on the Ward
oneLiner: Oliguria is dangerously low urine output (<0.5 mL/kg/h) reflecting acute kidney injury; early recognition and cause-targeted management can prevent dialysis.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 72-year-old man on post-op day 1 after emergency laparotomy for a perforated duodenal ulcer is reviewed by the night FY1. His urine output over the last 6 hours is 80 mL (total), and he feels “dry”. Observations: HR 108, BP 92/58, RR 18, saturations 96% on room air, temperature 36.8°C. He has a central line with CVP 4 mmHg, and his serum creatinine has risen from 85 to 145 µmol/L over 24 hours. He is on IV piperacillin-tazobactam for peritonitis.

**Pattern clues dissected:**
- **Post-op hypotension & low CVP:** suggests hypovolaemia – prerenal oliguria
- **Rising creatinine:** confirms AKI (≥26.5 µmol/L rise in 48h)
- **Recent laparotomy + sepsis:** dual hit – hypoperfusion plus systemic inflammation → acute tubular necrosis (ATN) risk
- **Low urine output sustained 6h:** triggers KDIGO stage 1 criterion
- **Piperacillin-tazobactam:** potential tubulointerstitial nephritis (though less common)
- **Absence of flank pain, haematuria:** postrenal obstruction less likely (but still check bladder scan)

**Atypical presentations:**
- **“Silent” hyperkalaemia:** a patient with oliguria and K+ 6.8 but no ECG changes – only clue is reduced UO on chart.
- **Normal creatinine but oliguric:** early AKI – creatinine lags behind acute UO drop by 12–24h.
- **Polyuria followed by oliguria:** “pre-renal” state (e.g., over-aggressive diuresis in heart failure) can transition to intrinsic AKI if not corrected.
- **Post-operative “adequate” urine output but still oliguric for weight:** a small patient (50 kg) needs ≥25 mL/h; 20 mL/h seems “okay” but is actually oliguric.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Oliguria is when the kidneys are not making enough urine – less than half a millilitre per kilogram of body weight per hour, averaged over six or more hours. It is the earliest quantitative sign that the kidneys are in trouble, often before blood tests change.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** 10–15% of hospitalised patients develop acute kidney injury (AKI); oliguria is present in >60% of AKI stages 1–2.
- **Stakes:** unrecognised/untreated oliguria leads to hyperkalaemia, pulmonary oedema, uraemic bleeding, and need for renal replacement therapy (RRT). Each RRT session costs £1,500–2,000 and carries line complications.
- **Common pitfall:** assuming “it’s just pre-renal” or “the catheter is blocked” without systematic assessment leads to missed intrinsic AKI (e.g., acute interstitial nephritis from antibiotics) – which is reversible if caught early but can progress to fibrosis.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of how it develops:**  
Oliguria is the final common pathway of three broad mechanisms: decreased renal perfusion (prerenal), structural damage to renal tubules/interstitium (intrinsic), or obstruction to urine flow (postrenal). The kidney is exquisitely sensitive to hypoperfusion because it receives 20% of cardiac output to maintain high filtration pressure. When mean arterial pressure falls below 60–70 mmHg (or vasoconstriction dominates), glomerular filtration drops – and urine output falls as a compensatory attempt to conserve volume. If hypoperfusion persists >30–60 minutes, tubular cells switch to anaerobic metabolism, ATP runs out, and they die (acute tubular necrosis, ATN). Necrotic cells slough into the lumen, casts form, back-leak of filtrate occurs, and urine output paradoxically falls further even after perfusion is restored.

**Why each key sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Mechanism | Clinical Sign | Why |
|-----------|----------------|-----|
| Decreased GFR → reduced filtrate | Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h) | Direct consequence of low perfusion pressure or blocked tubules |
| Renin-angiotensin activation → Na retention | Low urine Na (<20 mmol/L) in prerenal, high (>40) in ATN | Prerenal: tubular cells are intact and reabsorb Na. ATN: damaged tubules cannot reabsorb Na |
| Tubular injury → inability to concentrate urine | Urine osmolality <350 mOsm/kg in intrinsic AKI (vs >500 in prerenal) | Intact medullary gradient needed for countercurrent multiplication; lost in ATN |
| Reduced GFR → retained urea and creatinine | Rising serum Cr and urea | Filtration failure → accumulation of nitrogenous wastes |
| Loss of medullary concentrating ability | Fixed urine specific gravity (~1.010) in intrinsic AKI | Impaired ADH response or medullary damage |

**Pathophysiological connection to pattern cues:**  
- The septic post-op patient with hypotension has prerenal failure (low CVP, low urine Na initially) that, if untreated, progresses to ATN (fixed specific gravity, granular casts on urine microscopy).
- The rising creatinine despite normalising BP suggests tubular necrosis has already occurred.
- The absence of flank pain makes postrenal obstruction unlikely, but a distended bladder on palpation (or by bladder scan) must still be excluded (common forgotten cause in elderly men).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Cause | One-line “stem clue” |
|----------|--------------|-----------------------|
| **Pre-renal** (most common overall) | Hypovolaemia (dehydration, haemorrhage, burns) | “Low JVP, dry mucous membranes, postural hypotension” |
| | Sepsis (vasoplegia, third-spacing) | “Fever, tachypnoea, hypotension despite fluid” |
| | Heart failure (reduced effective circulating volume) | “Elevated JVP, peripheral oedema, bibasal crackles” |
| | Liver cirrhosis (hepatorenal syndrome) | “Ascites, jaundice, low urine Na” |
| **Intrinsic renal** | Acute tubular necrosis (ischaemic or nephrotoxic) | “Post-op sepsis/aminoglycosides/contrast – muddy brown granular casts in urine” |
| | Acute interstitial nephritis (drug-induced) | “Eosinophiluria, sterile pyuria, rash, fever – recent NSAID/penicillin/PPI” |
| | Glomerulonephritis (rapidly progressive) | “Haematuria, proteinuria, red cell casts, hypertension, oedema” |
| | Cortical necrosis / vascular (rare) | “Extensive shock – anuria, DIC” |
| **Post-renal** | Bladder outlet obstruction (BPH, clot, neurogenic) | “Distended bladder, suprapubic pain, or overflow incontinence – relieved by catheterisation” |
| | Ureteric obstruction (stones, retroperitoneal fibrosis) | “Colicky flank pain, haematuria, no response to catheter – US shows hydronephrosis” |

**Highlight the most common cause:** Pre-renal (hypovolaemia) in hospitalised patients – often iatrogenic (NBM, diuretics, laxatives). Must be distinguished from intrinsic ATN because management is opposite (fluids vs cautious fluid restriction).
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** According to **KDIGO (Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes)** – any ONE of:
- Serum creatinine rise ≥26.5 µmol/L within 48h
- Serum creatinine rise ≥1.5× baseline within 7 days
- Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥6 hours (Stage 1)

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Urine dipstick** | Protein, blood, glucose, leucocytes, nitrites | Screen for GN, infection, haemolysis | Blood + protein + RBC casts → glomerulonephritis; Leucocytes + nitrites → UTI/pyelonephritis |
| **Urine microscopy** | Casts, cells, crystals | Differentiate pre-renal from intrinsic AKI | Granular/muddy brown casts → ATN; RBC casts → GN; WBC casts → AIN**
| **U&E, creatinine** | Na, K, urea, Cr | Staging AKI, detecting hyperkalaemia, estimating GFR | Cr doubling = 50% GFR loss. High urea/Cr ratio >20:1 suggests prerenal |
| **Fractional excretion of Na (FeNa)** | Na in urine + serum + Cr | Differentiate prerenal vs intrinsic AKI | FeNa <1% = prerenal; >2% = intrinsic ATN (but unreliable in diuretic use) |
| **Renal ultrasound** | Kidney size, hydronephrosis, stones | Rule out postrenal obstruction | Hydronephrosis + dilated ureter = obstruction – needs urgent decompression (nephrostomy/stent) |
| **Chest X-ray** | Pulmonary oedema, infiltrates | Assess fluid overload if oliguric with dyspnoea | Bilateral fluffy shadows = fluid overload; consider RRT |
| **CK (creatine kinase)** | Muscle breakdown | Suspected rhabdomyolysis (crush, statin, seizure) | CK >5000 IU/L → ATN risk – forced alkaline diuresis or RRT |
| **Blood gas + lactate** | Metabolic acidosis, lactate, K+ | Emergent indication for RRT? | pH <7.2, K+ >6.5 lactate >4 mmol/L despite medical Rx – call ICU for RRT |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out**
- **Confirms postrenal obstruction:** hydronephrosis on ultrasound (bilateral or unilateral with solitary kidney)
- **Confirms ATN:** muddy brown granular casts on urine microscopy
- **Rarely obstruction:** complete anuria (<100 mL/24h) is characteristic of cortical necrosis or complete bilateral obstruction – needs immediate US
- **Rules out pre-renal (if present):** FeNa >2% in a patient not on diuretics (suggests intrinsic damage)
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) — what + why**
1. **Stop all nephrotoxins:** NSAIDs, ACE-inhibitors, ARBs, aminoglycosides (unless life-saving), IV contrast (defer if possible). *Why: reduce ongoing tubular insult.*
2. **Bladder scan or catheter:** measure residual volume; if >200 mL, insert urinary catheter (postrenal relief). *Why: rapid reversal of obstruction restores urine output within minutes.*
3. **Fluid challenge** (if no signs of fluid overload):  
   - Give 250–500 mL crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) IV over 15–30 min.  
   - Reassess after 30 min: if UO >0.5 mL/kg/h, continue fluids (total 1–2 L over 4h).  
   - If no response or CVP rises >8 mmHg → stop fluids; consider inotropes (norepinephrine) if hypotension persists.  
   *Why: pre-renal is volume-responsive; intrinsic is not.*  
4. **Check blood gas:** for K+, pH, lactate.  
5. **Treat hyperkalaemia immediately** if K+ >6.0 or ECG changes:  
   - Calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% IV over 2 min (membrane stabiliser).  
   - Actrapid insulin 10 units + 50 mL of 50% glucose IV (drives K+ into cells).  
   - Salbutamol nebulised 10 mg (optional additive).  
   - Consider calcium resonium or patiromer but onset >4h.  
   *Why: hyperkalaemia causes cardiac arrest if missed.*

**Ward (24–72h) — what + why**
- **Daily weight + strict fluid balance:** input/output + insensible losses. Target euvolemia. *Why: avoid fluid overload.*
- **Monitor UO hourly:** if <0.5 mL/kg/h for >12h, escalate.  
- **Continue fluid therapy:** if prerenal aetiology confirmed, maintain with 0.9% saline or balanced crystalloid at 20–30 mL/kg/24h titrated to haemodynamics.  
- **If ATN suspected:** fluid restrict to losses + 500 mL/day (oliguric phase). Avoid potassium and phosphate-containing fluids.  
- **Adjust drug doses:** CrCl <30 mL/min → many drugs need reduction (e.g., morphine, enoxaparin, antibiotics). Check BNF.  
- **Consider loop diuretics (furosemide)** only after optimising volume and BP – not routine. *Why: can convert oliguric to non-oliguric AKI which may avoid dialysis but does not improve survival.* Dose: furosemide 40–80 mg IV bolus (if euvolemic) or 10–40 mg/h infusion if needed.

**Long-term — what + why**
- **Identify and treat the cause:** sepsis → antibiotics; hepatorenal → terlipressin + albumin; GN → immunosuppression.  
- **Renal replacement therapy (RRT) triggers:**  
   - Refractory volume overload (pulmonary oedema)  
   - Persistent hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.5 despite medical therapy)  
   - Severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.15)  
   - Uraemic symptoms (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding)  
   - Rapidly rising urea/Cr (e.g., >20 mmol/L/day)  
- **Nutrition:** protein restriction is controversial – avoid catabolism. Consider enteral or parenteral nutrition early.  
- **Follow-up:** nephrology clinic at 3 months if AKI persists or eGFR <60.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**
- **Don’t give a large fluid bolus to a patient with heart failure or established ATN** → precipitates pulmonary oedema. Always assess JVP, lung bases, and CVP (if available) first.  
- **Don’t assume oliguria = hypovolaemia without checking FeNa/bladder/US** → 20% of inpatients have obstruction.  
- **Don’t prescribe IV gentamicin empirically without checking renal function daily** – nephrotoxic synergy with vancomycin.  
- **Don’t delay calling for RRT** while hoping medical therapy works – severe acidosis/hyperkalaemia can kill in hours.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Pre-renal AKI (Oliguria) | Intrinsic ATN | Postrenal Obstruction | Acute Interstitial Nephritis |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Urine output** | <0.5 mL/kg/h (often responds to fluids) | <0.5 mL/kg/h (rarely responds) | Anuria or fluctuating output | Usually oliguric, but may be non-oliguric |
| **Pain** | None or mild (hypotension) | None (unless associated muscle pain in rhabdomyolysis) | Colicky flank pain (stone); suprapubic distension (bladder outlet) | Flank pain occasional (10%) |
| **Exam** | Dry mucous membranes, low JVP, tachycardia | ± JVP normal/low; may have sepsis signs | Palpable bladder, prostate enlargement, or flank tenderness | Fever, maculopapular rash, arthralgias |
| **Urine dip/microscopy** | No casts; high specific gravity >1.020 | Muddy brown granular casts, renal tubular epithelial cells; low specific gravity ~1.010 | Normal sediment; may have RBCs if stone | WBC casts, eosinophiluria, proteinuria (mild) |
| **FeNa** | <1% (unless on diuretics) | >2% | Variable (<1% early, >2% late if ATN develops) | <1% early, >2% with prolonged damage |
| **Renal U/S** | Normal size, no hydronephrosis | Normal or enlarged + increase echogenicity | Hydronephrosis + dilated ureter (obstruction level) | Normal or enlarged |
| **Key differentiator** | Response to 500 mL fluid challenge (UO ↑) | Urine microscopy: granular casts | Bladder scan >200 mL residual → catheterisation restores flow | Eosinophilia + eosinophiluria + drug history (recent) |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Hyperkalaemia** | Ascending weakness, paraesthesia, then cardiac arrest | ECG: tented T waves, widened QRS, sine wave; K+ >6.5 | Emergency treatment (calcium, insulin+glucose) + call for RRT |
| **Pulmonary oedema** | Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, pink frothy sputum | Crackles up to apices, raised JVP, CXR bat-wing opacities | Fluid restrict, IV furosemide 40–80 mg IV, consider non-invasive ventilation + RRT |
| **Uraemic pericarditis** | Chest pain worsened by lying flat, fever | Pericardial rub (scratchy), ST elevation diffuse, effusion on echo | Urgent RRT (haemodialysis) – relieves within 24h |
| **Metabolic acidosis (high anion gap)** | Kussmaul breathing, confusion, hypotension | pH <7.2, HCO₃ <15, anion gap >20 | IV sodium bicarbonate (if pH <7.15) but caution with volume; RRT if refractory |
| **Uraemic bleeding** | Persistent oozing from lines, purpura, GI bleeding | Prolonged bleeding time due to platelet dysfunction | Desmopressin 0.3 µg/kg IV (temporarily improves platelet function), then RRT |
| **Refractory volume overload** | Persistent anuria despite catheter, worsening oxygen | CXR pulmonary oedema, BNP >2000 pg/mL | Urgent RRT – haemodialysis or haemofiltration |

**Late-presentation patterns (what tests ask if untreated):**  
- A patient with known AKI develops anuria for 24h; you are called for haemoptysis, oozing from IV site, and stupor – uraemic pleuritis/pericarditis/encephalopathy – immediate RRT needed.  
- Fever + flank pain + oliguria after recent septicaemia – suspect acute papillary necrosis (sloughed papillae obstruct ureter).
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics**

1. **“RIFLE” for AKI severity** (old classification, still used conceptually):  
   - **R**isk: Cr ↑ 1.5× or UO <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6h  
   - **I**njury: Cr ↑ 2× or UO <0.5 for 12h  
   - **F**ailure: Cr ↑ 3× or UO <0.3 for 24h or anuria 12h  
   - **L**oss: persistent AKI >4 weeks  
   - **E**nd-stage: >3 months  
   (Current KDIGO uses stages 1–3 but same principles.)

2. **“Are You OK?”** – Causes of Intrinsic AKI:  
   - **A**cute tubular necrosis  
   - **Y**ascular (vasculitis, TTP/HUS)  
   - **O**bstruction (postrenal – but considered separately)  
   - **K**idney interstitium (acute interstitial nephritis)  
   - (Also: Glomerulonephritis – not in mnemonic, add as “G”)

3. **“PREDICT”** – management steps to avoid RRT:  
   - **P**revent further insult (stop nephrotoxins)  
   - **R**estore perfusion (fluids only if pre-renal)  
   - **E**valuate obstruction (US + bladder scan)  
   - **D**ose drugs correctly  
   - **I**nduce diuresis if fluid overloaded (furosemide)  
   - **C**ontrol K+ and pH  
   - **T**ake daily weights and adjust

**High-yield clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues)**

1. **“If the bladder scan is empty and the patient is hypotensive, give fluids – not furosemide.”**  
   The most common error: giving IV furosemide to “stimulate urine” when the patient is dry – this worsens AKI.

2. **In sepsis, oliguria can be present despite normal BP.**  
   Capillary leak and functional hypovolaemia are real – check lactate, CVP, and dynamic measures.

3. **Always look at the drug chart for hidden nephrotoxins: PPIs (acute interstitial nephritis), NSAIDs, ACEi, ARBs, loop diuretics (overuse → pre-renal), and colistin/vancomycin (ATN risk).**

4. **Urine output is MORE sensitive than creatinine for early AKI.**  
   A 12-hour period of <0.5 mL/kg/h catches AKI 24 hours before creatinine rises.

5. **FeNa is unreliable if the patient is on diuretics.** Use fractional excretion of urea (FeUrea) instead: <35% suggests prerenal, >35% intrinsic.

6. **Post-operative oliguria in the first 12h can be normal (ADH, pain, anaesthesia) – but if persists >12h + Cr rise, suspect ATN.**

7. **Bilateral hydronephrosis with normal bladder on US?** – Suspect retroperitoneal fibrosis or prostate cancer with ureteric involvement. Refer urology + CT.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid**

- Not charting hourly UO in a septic patient (requires catheter)
- Starting a fluid challenge without first checking for pulmonary oedema
- Ordering renal ultrasound urgently in all cases (costly; unnecessary if obstruction unlikely – only indicated if anuria, haematuria, flank pain, or history of stones)
- Assuming K+ >6.0 is a lab error – treat first, ask questions later
- Delaying nephrology referral until Cr >500 µmol/L – call early if oliguric >24h or RRT may be needed
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team**

- **NEWS2 ≥7 or any single criterion (RR <8 or >25, SpO₂ <91%, HR <40 or >130, SBP <90 or >220)** – immediate call to crash team.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** despite initial fluid resuscitation – sepsis or hypoperfusion needing ICU.
- **GCS <13 or acute confusion** – uraemic encephalopathy or other cause.
- **K+ >6.5 mmol/L** or any ECG change (wide QRS, sine wave) – emergency treatment + call for RRT (Nephrology/ICU).
- **Anuria (<100 mL/24h) for >12h** – high risk of complete obstruction or cortical necrosis; call senior + arrange urgent US.
- **pH <7.15 despite bicarbonate therapy** – call ICU for haemofiltration.
- **Severe pulmonary oedema** – persistent O₂ requirement >15 L/min or NIV needed; call ICU for RRT.
- **Uraemic pericarditis or bleeding** – immediate RRT needed; call on-call Nephrology.
- **If patient is pregnant or <16 years old** – involve obstetrics/paediatrics and senior.

**Specific thresholds to use when calling:**

- “I have a patient with oliguria (UO 0.2 mL/kg/h for 8h), creatinine rose from 80 to 220, K+ 6.2 with tall T waves, and NEWS2 6. I’ve given calcium and insulin/glucose. Please come and review for possible RRT.”  
- “The bladder scan was empty, we gave a 500 mL fluid challenge but no increase in UO. FeNa is 3% – likely intrinsic ATN. Shall we start fluid restriction and call Nephrology?”

Remember: **Oliguria is a red flag, not a lab value. Treat the cause, not the number. If you’re worried, escalate early – the team would rather be called too soon than too late.**

## Oliguria on the Ward

**One-liner:** Oliguria is dangerously low urine output (<0.5 mL/kg/h) reflecting acute kidney injury; early recognition and cause-targeted management can prevent dialysis.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 72-year-old man on post-op day 1 after emergency laparotomy for a perforated duodenal ulcer is reviewed by the night FY1. His urine output over the last 6 hours is 80 mL (total), and he feels “dry”. Observations: HR 108, BP 92/58, RR 18, saturations 96% on room air, temperature 36.8°C. He has a central line with CVP 4 mmHg, and his serum creatinine has risen from 85 to 145 µmol/L over 24 hours. He is on IV piperacillin-tazobactam for peritonitis.

**Pattern clues dissected:**
- **Post-op hypotension & low CVP:** suggests hypovolaemia – prerenal oliguria
- **Rising creatinine:** confirms AKI (≥26.5 µmol/L rise in 48h)
- **Recent laparotomy + sepsis:** dual hit – hypoperfusion plus systemic inflammation → acute tubular necrosis (ATN) risk
- **Low urine output sustained 6h:** triggers KDIGO stage 1 criterion
- **Piperacillin-tazobactam:** potential tubulointerstitial nephritis (though less common)
- **Absence of flank pain, haematuria:** postrenal obstruction less likely (but still check bladder scan)

**Atypical presentations:**
- **“Silent” hyperkalaemia:** a patient with oliguria and K+ 6.8 but no ECG changes – only clue is reduced UO on chart.
- **Normal creatinine but oliguric:** early AKI – creatinine lags behind acute UO drop by 12–24h.
- **Polyuria followed by oliguria:** “pre-renal” state (e.g., over-aggressive diuresis in heart failure) can transition to intrinsic AKI if not corrected.
- **Post-operative “adequate” urine output but still oliguric for weight:** a small patient (50 kg) needs ≥25 mL/h; 20 mL/h seems “okay” but is actually oliguric.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Oliguria is when the kidneys are not making enough urine – less than half a millilitre per kilogram of body weight per hour, averaged over six or more hours. It is the earliest quantitative sign that the kidneys are in trouble, often before blood tests change.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** 10–15% of hospitalised patients develop acute kidney injury (AKI); oliguria is present in >60% of AKI stages 1–2.
- **Stakes:** unrecognised/untreated oliguria leads to hyperkalaemia, pulmonary oedema, uraemic bleeding, and need for renal replacement therapy (RRT). Each RRT session costs £1,500–2,000 and carries line complications.
- **Common pitfall:** assuming “it’s just pre-renal” or “the catheter is blocked” without systematic assessment leads to missed intrinsic AKI (e.g., acute interstitial nephritis from antibiotics) – which is reversible if caught early but can progress to fibrosis.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of how it develops:**  
Oliguria is the final common pathway of three broad mechanisms: decreased renal perfusion (prerenal), structural damage to renal tubules/interstitium (intrinsic), or obstruction to urine flow (postrenal). The kidney is exquisitely sensitive to hypoperfusion because it receives 20% of cardiac output to maintain high filtration pressure. When mean arterial pressure falls below 60–70 mmHg (or vasoconstriction dominates), glomerular filtration drops – and urine output falls as a compensatory attempt to conserve volume. If hypoperfusion persists >30–60 minutes, tubular cells switch to anaerobic metabolism, ATP runs out, and they die (acute tubular necrosis, ATN). Necrotic cells slough into the lumen, casts form, back-leak of filtrate occurs, and urine output paradoxically falls further even after perfusion is restored.

**Why each key sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Mechanism | Clinical Sign | Why |
|-----------|----------------|-----|
| Decreased GFR → reduced filtrate | Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h) | Direct consequence of low perfusion pressure or blocked tubules |
| Renin-angiotensin activation → Na retention | Low urine Na (<20 mmol/L) in prerenal, high (>40) in ATN | Prerenal: tubular cells are intact and reabsorb Na. ATN: damaged tubules cannot reabsorb Na |
| Tubular injury → inability to concentrate urine | Urine osmolality <350 mOsm/kg in intrinsic AKI (vs >500 in prerenal) | Intact medullary gradient needed for countercurrent multiplication; lost in ATN |
| Reduced GFR → retained urea and creatinine | Rising serum Cr and urea | Filtration failure → accumulation of nitrogenous wastes |
| Loss of medullary concentrating ability | Fixed urine specific gravity (~1.010) in intrinsic AKI | Impaired ADH response or medullary damage |

**Pathophysiological connection to pattern cues:**  
- The septic post-op patient with hypotension has prerenal failure (low CVP, low urine Na initially) that, if untreated, progresses to ATN (fixed specific gravity, granular casts on urine microscopy).
- The rising creatinine despite normalising BP suggests tubular necrosis has already occurred.
- The absence of flank pain makes postrenal obstruction unlikely, but a distended bladder on palpation (or by bladder scan) must still be excluded (common forgotten cause in elderly men).

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Common Cause | One-line “stem clue” |
|----------|--------------|-----------------------|
| **Pre-renal** (most common overall) | Hypovolaemia (dehydration, haemorrhage, burns) | “Low JVP, dry mucous membranes, postural hypotension” |
| | Sepsis (vasoplegia, third-spacing) | “Fever, tachypnoea, hypotension despite fluid” |
| | Heart failure (reduced effective circulating volume) | “Elevated JVP, peripheral oedema, bibasal crackles” |
| | Liver cirrhosis (hepatorenal syndrome) | “Ascites, jaundice, low urine Na” |
| **Intrinsic renal** | Acute tubular necrosis (ischaemic or nephrotoxic) | “Post-op sepsis/aminoglycosides/contrast – muddy brown granular casts in urine” |
| | Acute interstitial nephritis (drug-induced) | “Eosinophiluria, sterile pyuria, rash, fever – recent NSAID/penicillin/PPI” |
| | Glomerulonephritis (rapidly progressive) | “Haematuria, proteinuria, red cell casts, hypertension, oedema” |
| | Cortical necrosis / vascular (rare) | “Extensive shock – anuria, DIC” |
| **Post-renal** | Bladder outlet obstruction (BPH, clot, neurogenic) | “Distended bladder, suprapubic pain, or overflow incontinence – relieved by catheterisation” |
| | Ureteric obstruction (stones, retroperitoneal fibrosis) | “Colicky flank pain, haematuria, no response to catheter – US shows hydronephrosis” |

**Highlight the most common cause:** Pre-renal (hypovolaemia) in hospitalised patients – often iatrogenic (NBM, diuretics, laxatives). Must be distinguished from intrinsic ATN because management is opposite (fluids vs cautious fluid restriction).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** According to **KDIGO (Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes)** – any ONE of:
- Serum creatinine rise ≥26.5 µmol/L within 48h
- Serum creatinine rise ≥1.5× baseline within 7 days
- Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥6 hours (Stage 1)

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Urine dipstick** | Protein, blood, glucose, leucocytes, nitrites | Screen for GN, infection, haemolysis | Blood + protein + RBC casts → glomerulonephritis; Leucocytes + nitrites → UTI/pyelonephritis |
| **Urine microscopy** | Casts, cells, crystals | Differentiate pre-renal from intrinsic AKI | Granular/muddy brown casts → ATN; RBC casts → GN; WBC casts → AIN**
| **U&E, creatinine** | Na, K, urea, Cr | Staging AKI, detecting hyperkalaemia, estimating GFR | Cr doubling = 50% GFR loss. High urea/Cr ratio >20:1 suggests prerenal |
| **Fractional excretion of Na (FeNa)** | Na in urine + serum + Cr | Differentiate prerenal vs intrinsic AKI | FeNa <1% = prerenal; >2% = intrinsic ATN (but unreliable in diuretic use) |
| **Renal ultrasound** | Kidney size, hydronephrosis, stones | Rule out postrenal obstruction | Hydronephrosis + dilated ureter = obstruction – needs urgent decompression (nephrostomy/stent) |
| **Chest X-ray** | Pulmonary oedema, infiltrates | Assess fluid overload if oliguric with dyspnoea | Bilateral fluffy shadows = fluid overload; consider RRT |
| **CK (creatine kinase)** | Muscle breakdown | Suspected rhabdomyolysis (crush, statin, seizure) | CK >5000 IU/L → ATN risk – forced alkaline diuresis or RRT |
| **Blood gas + lactate** | Metabolic acidosis, lactate, K+ | Emergent indication for RRT? | pH <7.2, K+ >6.5 lactate >4 mmol/L despite medical Rx – call ICU for RRT |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out**
- **Confirms postrenal obstruction:** hydronephrosis on ultrasound (bilateral or unilateral with solitary kidney)
- **Confirms ATN:** muddy brown granular casts on urine microscopy
- **Rarely obstruction:** complete anuria (<100 mL/24h) is characteristic of cortical necrosis or complete bilateral obstruction – needs immediate US
- **Rules out pre-renal (if present):** FeNa >2% in a patient not on diuretics (suggests intrinsic damage)

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) — what + why**
1. **Stop all nephrotoxins:** NSAIDs, ACE-inhibitors, ARBs, aminoglycosides (unless life-saving), IV contrast (defer if possible). *Why: reduce ongoing tubular insult.*
2. **Bladder scan or catheter:** measure residual volume; if >200 mL, insert urinary catheter (postrenal relief). *Why: rapid reversal of obstruction restores urine output within minutes.*
3. **Fluid challenge** (if no signs of fluid overload):  
   - Give 250–500 mL crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) IV over 15–30 min.  
   - Reassess after 30 min: if UO >0.5 mL/kg/h, continue fluids (total 1–2 L over 4h).  
   - If no response or CVP rises >8 mmHg → stop fluids; consider inotropes (norepinephrine) if hypotension persists.  
   *Why: pre-renal is volume-responsive; intrinsic is not.*  
4. **Check blood gas:** for K+, pH, lactate.  
5. **Treat hyperkalaemia immediately** if K+ >6.0 or ECG changes:  
   - Calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% IV over 2 min (membrane stabiliser).  
   - Actrapid insulin 10 units + 50 mL of 50% glucose IV (drives K+ into cells).  
   - Salbutamol nebulised 10 mg (optional additive).  
   - Consider calcium resonium or patiromer but onset >4h.  
   *Why: hyperkalaemia causes cardiac arrest if missed.*

**Ward (24–72h) — what + why**
- **Daily weight + strict fluid balance:** input/output + insensible losses. Target euvolemia. *Why: avoid fluid overload.*
- **Monitor UO hourly:** if <0.5 mL/kg/h for >12h, escalate.  
- **Continue fluid therapy:** if prerenal aetiology confirmed, maintain with 0.9% saline or balanced crystalloid at 20–30 mL/kg/24h titrated to haemodynamics.  
- **If ATN suspected:** fluid restrict to losses + 500 mL/day (oliguric phase). Avoid potassium and phosphate-containing fluids.  
- **Adjust drug doses:** CrCl <30 mL/min → many drugs need reduction (e.g., morphine, enoxaparin, antibiotics). Check BNF.  
- **Consider loop diuretics (furosemide)** only after optimising volume and BP – not routine. *Why: can convert oliguric to non-oliguric AKI which may avoid dialysis but does not improve survival.* Dose: furosemide 40–80 mg IV bolus (if euvolemic) or 10–40 mg/h infusion if needed.

**Long-term — what + why**
- **Identify and treat the cause:** sepsis → antibiotics; hepatorenal → terlipressin + albumin; GN → immunosuppression.  
- **Renal replacement therapy (RRT) triggers:**  
   - Refractory volume overload (pulmonary oedema)  
   - Persistent hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.5 despite medical therapy)  
   - Severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.15)  
   - Uraemic symptoms (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding)  
   - Rapidly rising urea/Cr (e.g., >20 mmol/L/day)  
- **Nutrition:** protein restriction is controversial – avoid catabolism. Consider enteral or parenteral nutrition early.  
- **Follow-up:** nephrology clinic at 3 months if AKI persists or eGFR <60.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**
- **Don’t give a large fluid bolus to a patient with heart failure or established ATN** → precipitates pulmonary oedema. Always assess JVP, lung bases, and CVP (if available) first.  
- **Don’t assume oliguria = hypovolaemia without checking FeNa/bladder/US** → 20% of inpatients have obstruction.  
- **Don’t prescribe IV gentamicin empirically without checking renal function daily** – nephrotoxic synergy with vancomycin.  
- **Don’t delay calling for RRT** while hoping medical therapy works – severe acidosis/hyperkalaemia can kill in hours.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Pre-renal AKI (Oliguria) | Intrinsic ATN | Postrenal Obstruction | Acute Interstitial Nephritis |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Urine output** | <0.5 mL/kg/h (often responds to fluids) | <0.5 mL/kg/h (rarely responds) | Anuria or fluctuating output | Usually oliguric, but may be non-oliguric |
| **Pain** | None or mild (hypotension) | None (unless associated muscle pain in rhabdomyolysis) | Colicky flank pain (stone); suprapubic distension (bladder outlet) | Flank pain occasional (10%) |
| **Exam** | Dry mucous membranes, low JVP, tachycardia | ± JVP normal/low; may have sepsis signs | Palpable bladder, prostate enlargement, or flank tenderness | Fever, maculopapular rash, arthralgias |
| **Urine dip/microscopy** | No casts; high specific gravity >1.020 | Muddy brown granular casts, renal tubular epithelial cells; low specific gravity ~1.010 | Normal sediment; may have RBCs if stone | WBC casts, eosinophiluria, proteinuria (mild) |
| **FeNa** | <1% (unless on diuretics) | >2% | Variable (<1% early, >2% late if ATN develops) | <1% early, >2% with prolonged damage |
| **Renal U/S** | Normal size, no hydronephrosis | Normal or enlarged + increase echogenicity | Hydronephrosis + dilated ureter (obstruction level) | Normal or enlarged |
| **Key differentiator** | Response to 500 mL fluid challenge (UO ↑) | Urine microscopy: granular casts | Bladder scan >200 mL residual → catheterisation restores flow | Eosinophilia + eosinophiluria + drug history (recent) |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Hyperkalaemia** | Ascending weakness, paraesthesia, then cardiac arrest | ECG: tented T waves, widened QRS, sine wave; K+ >6.5 | Emergency treatment (calcium, insulin+glucose) + call for RRT |
| **Pulmonary oedema** | Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, pink frothy sputum | Crackles up to apices, raised JVP, CXR bat-wing opacities | Fluid restrict, IV furosemide 40–80 mg IV, consider non-invasive ventilation + RRT |
| **Uraemic pericarditis** | Chest pain worsened by lying flat, fever | Pericardial rub (scratchy), ST elevation diffuse, effusion on echo | Urgent RRT (haemodialysis) – relieves within 24h |
| **Metabolic acidosis (high anion gap)** | Kussmaul breathing, confusion, hypotension | pH <7.2, HCO₃ <15, anion gap >20 | IV sodium bicarbonate (if pH <7.15) but caution with volume; RRT if refractory |
| **Uraemic bleeding** | Persistent oozing from lines, purpura, GI bleeding | Prolonged bleeding time due to platelet dysfunction | Desmopressin 0.3 µg/kg IV (temporarily improves platelet function), then RRT |
| **Refractory volume overload** | Persistent anuria despite catheter, worsening oxygen | CXR pulmonary oedema, BNP >2000 pg/mL | Urgent RRT – haemodialysis or haemofiltration |

**Late-presentation patterns (what tests ask if untreated):**  
- A patient with known AKI develops anuria for 24h; you are called for haemoptysis, oozing from IV site, and stupor – uraemic pleuritis/pericarditis/encephalopathy – immediate RRT needed.  
- Fever + flank pain + oliguria after recent septicaemia – suspect acute papillary necrosis (sloughed papillae obstruct ureter).  

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics**

1. **“RIFLE” for AKI severity** (old classification, still used conceptually):  
   - **R**isk: Cr ↑ 1.5× or UO <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6h  
   - **I**njury: Cr ↑ 2× or UO <0.5 for 12h  
   - **F**ailure: Cr ↑ 3× or UO <0.3 for 24h or anuria 12h  
   - **L**oss: persistent AKI >4 weeks  
   - **E**nd-stage: >3 months  
   (Current KDIGO uses stages 1–3 but same principles.)

2. **“Are You OK?”** – Causes of Intrinsic AKI:  
   - **A**cute tubular necrosis  
   - **Y**ascular (vasculitis, TTP/HUS)  
   - **O**bstruction (postrenal – but considered separately)  
   - **K**idney interstitium (acute interstitial nephritis)  
   - (Also: Glomerulonephritis – not in mnemonic, add as “G”)

3. **“PREDICT”** – management steps to avoid RRT:  
   - **P**revent further insult (stop nephrotoxins)  
   - **R**estore perfusion (fluids only if pre-renal)  
   - **E**valuate obstruction (US + bladder scan)  
   - **D**ose drugs correctly  
   - **I**nduce diuresis if fluid overloaded (furosemide)  
   - **C**ontrol K+ and pH  
   - **T**ake daily weights and adjust

**High-yield clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues)**

1. **“If the bladder scan is empty and the patient is hypotensive, give fluids – not furosemide.”**  
   The most common error: giving IV furosemide to “stimulate urine” when the patient is dry – this worsens AKI.

2. **In sepsis, oliguria can be present despite normal BP.**  
   Capillary leak and functional hypovolaemia are real – check lactate, CVP, and dynamic measures.

3. **Always look at the drug chart for hidden nephrotoxins: PPIs (acute interstitial nephritis), NSAIDs, ACEi, ARBs, loop diuretics (overuse → pre-renal), and colistin/vancomycin (ATN risk).**

4. **Urine output is MORE sensitive than creatinine for early AKI.**  
   A 12-hour period of <0.5 mL/kg/h catches AKI 24 hours before creatinine rises.

5. **FeNa is unreliable if the patient is on diuretics.** Use fractional excretion of urea (FeUrea) instead: <35% suggests prerenal, >35% intrinsic.

6. **Post-operative oliguria in the first 12h can be normal (ADH, pain, anaesthesia) – but if persists >12h + Cr rise, suspect ATN.**

7. **Bilateral hydronephrosis with normal bladder on US?** – Suspect retroperitoneal fibrosis or prostate cancer with ureteric involvement. Refer urology + CT.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid**

- Not charting hourly UO in a septic patient (requires catheter)
- Starting a fluid challenge without first checking for pulmonary oedema
- Ordering renal ultrasound urgently in all cases (costly; unnecessary if obstruction unlikely – only indicated if anuria, haematuria, flank pain, or history of stones)
- Assuming K+ >6.0 is a lab error – treat first, ask questions later
- Delaying nephrology referral until Cr >500 µmol/L – call early if oliguric >24h or RRT may be needed

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / crash team**

- **NEWS2 ≥7 or any single criterion (RR <8 or >25, SpO₂ <91%, HR <40 or >130, SBP <90 or >220)** – immediate call to crash team.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** despite initial fluid resuscitation – sepsis or hypoperfusion needing ICU.
- **GCS <13 or acute confusion** – uraemic encephalopathy or other cause.
- **K+ >6.5 mmol/L** or any ECG change (wide QRS, sine wave) – emergency treatment + call for RRT (Nephrology/ICU).
- **Anuria (<100 mL/24h) for >12h** – high risk of complete obstruction or cortical necrosis; call senior + arrange urgent US.
- **pH <7.15 despite bicarbonate therapy** – call ICU for haemofiltration.
- **Severe pulmonary oedema** – persistent O₂ requirement >15 L/min or NIV needed; call ICU for RRT.
- **Uraemic pericarditis or bleeding** – immediate RRT needed; call on-call Nephrology.
- **If patient is pregnant or <16 years old** – involve obstetrics/paediatrics and senior.

**Specific thresholds to use when calling:**

- “I have a patient with oliguria (UO 0.2 mL/kg/h for 8h), creatinine rose from 80 to 220, K+ 6.2 with tall T waves, and NEWS2 6. I’ve given calcium and insulin/glucose. Please come and review for possible RRT.”  
- “The bladder scan was empty, we gave a 500 mL fluid challenge but no increase in UO. FeNa is 3% – likely intrinsic ATN. Shall we start fluid restriction and call Nephrology?”

Remember: **Oliguria is a red flag, not a lab value. Treat the cause, not the number. If you’re worried, escalate early – the team would rather be called too soon than too late.**
